title: d and f Block Elements Test - 2 (Topic Test)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText:
Given below are two statement : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R :

Assertion A : Size of Bk3+ ion is less than Np3+ ion.

Reason R : The above is a consequence of the lanthanoid contraction.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both A and B are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
Both A and B are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
questions.solution:
Size of Bk3+ is 98 pm

Size of Np3+ is 101 pm

So size of Np3+ is more than Bk3+ ion.

there is a gradual decrease in the size of M3+ ions across the series. This may be referred to as the actinoid contraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Preparation of potassium permanganate from $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ involves two step process in which the 1st step is a reaction with KOH and $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ to produce
questions.solution:
The preparation of potassium permanganate ($\mathrm{KMnO}_4$) from manganese dioxide ($\mathrm{MnO}_2$) involves a two-step process. In the first step, $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ reacts with potassium hydroxide ($\mathrm{KOH}$) and potassium nitrate ($\mathrm{KNO}_3$) under heating. This reaction produces potassium manganate ($\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{MnO}_4$) as an intermediate product. The reaction can be depicted as follows:

$ \mathrm{MnO}_2 \xrightarrow[\mathrm{KNO}_3, \mathrm{\Delta}]{\mathrm{KOH}} \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{MnO}_4 $

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which one of the following species is stable in aqueous solution?
questions.solution:
MnO42− disproportionate as (in neutral or acidic solution)

3MnO42− + 4H+ → 2MnO42− + MnO2 + 2H2O

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: The $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ion, having five unpaired electrons, shows a spin-only magnetic moment closest to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ is $3 d^5$, all electrons unpaired (high-spin $d^5$ ): Spin-only moment

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{[n(n+2)]}=\sqrt{[5 \times 7]} \\ & =\sqrt{35} \approx 5.92 \mathrm{BM} \approx 6 \mathrm{BM} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Potassium permanganate on heating at 513 K gives a product which is :
questions.solution:
$$2KMn{O_4}\mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow} \limits_{200^\circ C}^\Delta } \mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{Green} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{Black} + {O_2}$$

In K2MnO4, manganese oxidation state is +6 and hence it has one unpaired e$$-$$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: With reference to aqua-regia, choose the correct option(s).
options: Aqua-regia is prepared by mixing conc. HCl and conc. HNO<sub>3</sub> in 3 : 1 (v / v) ratio
The yellow colour of aqua-regia is due to the presence of NOCl and Cl<sub>2</sub>
Reaction of gold with aqua-regia produces an anion having Au in +3 oxidation state
Reaction of gold with aqua regia produces NO<sub>2</sub> in the absence of air
questions.solution:
The explanation of given statements are as follows :

(a) Aqua-regia is prepared by mixing conc. HCl and conc. HNO3 in 3 : 1 (v/v) ratio and is used in oxidation of gold and platinum. Hence, option (a) is correct.

(b) Yellow colour of aqua-regia is due to its decomposition into NOCl (orange yellow) and Cl2 (greenish yellow). Hence, option (b) is correct.

(c) When gold reacts with aqua-regia then it produces $$AuCl_4^ - $$ anion complex in which Au has +3 oxidation state.



Hence, option (c) is correct.

(d) Reaction of gold with aqua-regia produces NO gas in absence of air. Hence, option (d) is incorrect.

questions.questionText: The value of magnetic quantum number of the outermost electron of Zn<sup>+</sup> ion is ______________.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which element is not present in Nessler's reagent?
questions.solution:
Nessler’s reagent is K2[HgI4]

Therefore, among the options given, oxygen is the only element that is not present in Nessler's reagent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText:
(a) Why is chemistry of actinoids complicated as compared to lanthanoids?

(b) Complete the following reaction and justify that it is a disproportionation reaction:

$$3 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow$$________ $$+$$ _________ $$+$$ $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$.

(c) The given graph shows the trends in melting points of transition metals:

Explain the reason why $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ has highest melting point and manganese $$(\mathrm{Mn})$$ a lower melting point.
questions.solution:
(a) Actinoids are present below the series of lanthanoid. They are radio active in nature. To study them is more complicated as compared to lanthanoid. Second, they have variable oxidation state.

(b) $$3 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+4 \mathrm{H}+\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+\mathrm{MnO}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$

It is disproportination reaction because oxidation take place in acidic medium and oxidation numbers are

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}=6 \\ & \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}=7 \\ & \mathrm{MnO}_2=4 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{(c)} \quad & \mathrm{Cr}=24[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d} 54 \mathrm{~s}^1 4 \\ & \mathrm{Mn}=25[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~s}^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{Cr}$$ has maximum number of unpaired electrons in $$\alpha$$-orbital. So it can have maximum pairing and form bond. Number of unpaired electrons are six in $$\mathrm{Cr}$$. But Mn has five unpaired electron in d-orbital which is less than Cr. That's why boiling point of $$\mathrm{Cr}$$ is maximum in transition metal. $$\mathrm{Mn}$$ is half filled which have extra stability and less tendency of form bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Which series of reactions correctly represents chemical relations related to iron and its compound?
options:
Fe $$\buildrel {{Cl_2} \, {, heat}} \over
 \longrightarrow $$ FeCl<sub>3</sub> $$\buildrel {heat, \, air} \over
 \longrightarrow $$ FeCl<sub>2</sub> $$\buildrel {Zn} \over
 \longrightarrow $$ Fe
questions.solution:
In equation

(i) $$\,\,\,$$ $$F{e_2}{\left( {S{O_4}} \right)_3}\,\,\,$$

and in equation

(ii) $$\,\,\,$$ $$F{e_2}{\left( {S{O_4}} \right)_3}$$

on decomposing will form oxide instead of $$Fe.$$

The correct sequence of reactions is

$$Fe\buildrel {{O_2},heat} \over \longrightarrow \,\,F{e_3}{O_4}\buildrel {Co,{{600}^ \circ }C} \over \longrightarrow $$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,F{e_2}{\left( {S{O_4}} \right)_3}\buildrel \Delta \over \longrightarrow Fe$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: $$\mathrm{KMnO}_4$$ decomposes on heating at $$513 \mathrm{~K}$$ to form $$\mathrm{O}_2$$ along with

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: In neutral or faintly alkaline medium, $$\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$$ being a powerful oxidant can oxidize, thiosulphate almost quantitatively, to sulphate. In this reaction overall change in oxidation state of manganese will be :
questions.solution:
In neutral or Faintly alkaline medium, thiosulphate is oxidised almost quantitatively to sulphate ion according to reaction given below,

$8 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+3 \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 8 \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$

Here the $\mathrm{Mn}$ changes from $\mathrm{Mn}^{+7}$ to $\mathrm{Mn}^{+4}$

Thus overall change in its oxidation number would be of 3 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.questionText: Upon heating with Cu<sub>2</sub>S, the reagent(s) that give copper metal is/are
questions.solution:
To understand which of the given reagents will give copper metal upon heating with $Cu_2S$, let's discuss each one based on their chemical reactions during the process:

Option A: CuFeS2

$CuFeS_2$ (Chalcopyrite) when heated with $Cu_2S$, it can react to form copper metal as part of the copper extraction process. However, the direct reaction between $CuFeS_2$ and $Cu_2S$ is more involved and typically requires a series of steps, including smelting and conversion to get to the copper metal. Therefore, while $CuFeS_2$ is a source of copper, it's not directly because of its reaction with $Cu_2S$, but through a complex metallurgical process.

Option B: CuO

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 \mathrm{CuO} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_2 \\\\ & \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_2 \end{aligned} $$

Option C: Cu2O

Copper(I) oxide ($Cu_2O$) can also react with $Cu_2S$ to produce copper metal directly. The equation for this reaction is:

$2Cu_2O + Cu_2S \rightarrow 6Cu + SO_2$

Here, $Cu_2O$ serves as the oxidizing agent, reducing $Cu_2S$ to copper metal, while itself being reduced to copper, and sulfur is oxidized to sulfur dioxide. This reaction is similar in principle to the reaction with $CuO$, but involves copper(I) oxide instead.

Option D: CuSO4

$\begin{array}{r}\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \xrightarrow{720^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{CuO}+\mathrm{SO}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \\\\ 4 \mathrm{CuO} \xrightarrow{1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_2 \\\\ 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_2\end{array}$
